item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My review from the Art Exchange forum:

This is a beautiful dog story. It's hard to go wrong with dog stories. Their simple minds and big hearts make them endearing protagonists immediately. I've already read the skip this is based on, and a graphic novel centered around Laika, so I'm already very invested in this adorable, tragic little dog. This delivers on that investment. A dog contemplating its own death is… not fun. And very well executed. Bravo.

Nothing to add. Bravo x2, and a +1 as well.

item.title: The Foundation meets real-world history
item.content:encoded: Love this kind of story — especially when you tell it from an unusual viewpoint like this. One upvote isn't enough!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has changed a decent amount over time, and I think all for the better. Overall, a very evocative and surprisingly heartwarming story. Nice work!

item.title: Barker: +1
item.content:encoded: I'm glad to bring this from the unfeeling zero into the lukewarm single digits. It's small and simple, but very effective within the short spread of the story, and the ending really made me feel something. Good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a short story about dogs and the things that they want, written for TyGently for the 2020 Secret Santa. There's a small reference to another Soviet dog, if you care to find it.

I know that the death is not historically accurate, but accounts have changed repeatedly over the years, so it's not completely out of the question. The record is regrettably silent on the matter of giant alien space gods and their demand for blood sacrifices, so I can't vouch for the accuracy of that aspect.

Thanks to Riemann, weizhong, and A Random Day for looking over and providing critique.
